treatments.name: Perioperative Pembrolizumab + Surgery + Adjuvant CRT (KEYNOTE-689 Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT (KEYNOTE-689) with 704 patients showed statistically significant EFS benefit (HR 0.73, p=0.0041). FDA approved June 2025 for perioperative use in PD-L1+ LA-HNSCC. First positive perioperative trial in HNSCC in 20+ years. Published in NEJM 2025.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median EFS 51.8 months vs 30.4 months with SOC alone. 36-month EFS 57.6% vs 46.4%. However, sinonasal subsites were a minority of enrolled patients and benefit may differ. OS data still maturing.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved (June 2025) for PD-L1-expressing resectable LA-HNSCC. Pembrolizumab widely available. Patient's PD-L1 CPS 10 meets eligibility threshold (CPS >= 1). Requires coordination of neoadjuvant dosing before surgery.

safety_profile.rationale: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab did not affect surgical completion rates. Immune-related adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, thyroiditis) occur in ~15-20% but mostly grade 1-2. Added to baseline surgical + CRT toxicity.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient's PD-L1 CPS 10 meets the CPS >= 1 threshold for approved indication. Benefit increases with higher CPS. TP53 mutation may reduce immunotherapy benefit per some analyses, but PD-L1 positivity is the validated biomarker.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab (2 cycles, 200 mg IV q3w) primes antitumor T-cell response before surgery by blocking PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, potentially converting immunologically cold tumors to hot. Adjuvant pembrolizumab (15 cycles) maintains immune surveillance post-surgery to eliminate micrometastatic disease. Combined with standard adjuvant RT +/- cisplatin.
notable_side_effects: potential wound healing delay if given neoadjuvantly (not observed in KEYNOTE-689)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (June 2025) — perioperative pembrolizumab for PD-L1+ resectable LA-HNSCC

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab + Platinum + 5-FU (KEYNOTE-048 Regimen for R/M Disease)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT (KEYNOTE-048, n=882) established as first-line standard for R/M HNSCC. 5-year follow-up confirms durable OS benefit. Published in Lancet and JCO with multiple updates. FDA approved for R/M HNSCC.

survival_benefit.rationale: Pembrolizumab + chemo: median OS 13.0 months vs 10.7 months (HR 0.71 in total population). In CPS >= 20: HR 0.62. 5-year OS 16% vs 5.2%. Applicable only in recurrent/metastatic setting, not curative intent for this patient's current stage.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved first-line for R/M HNSCC. Pembrolizumab widely available globally. This would be relevant as salvage therapy if primary treatment fails or disease recurs.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3-5 AEs in ~70% of patients on combination arm. Immune-related AEs (thyroiditis, colitis, hepatitis). Platinum toxicity. Treatment-related death rate ~4%. Better quality of life scores vs cetuximab-chemo despite similar AE rates.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient's PD-L1 CPS 10 falls in CPS >= 1 subgroup where significant benefit is demonstrated (HR 0.64). Pembrolizumab monotherapy preferred for CPS >= 20; combination for CPS 1-19 where this patient falls.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab blocks PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint restoring T-cell mediated antitumor immunity. Combined with platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin or carboplatin) plus 5-FU for 6 cycles, creating immunogenic cell death that synergizes with checkpoint blockade. Pembrolizumab continues as maintenance for up to 35 cycles.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — first-line R/M HNSCC (salvage setting for this patient)

treatments.name: Cetuximab + Radiation Therapy (Bioradiation — Cisplatin-Unfit Alternative)

evidence_level.rationale: Bonner trial (phase 3 RCT, n=424) established cetuximab + RT for LA-HNSCC. Cetuximab + radical RT for sinonasal SCC studied by Xiong et al. showing improved survival. PCE (paclitaxel/carboplatin/cetuximab) induction achieves 96% ORR in maxillary sinus SCC (PMC10823329).

survival_benefit.rationale: Cetuximab + RT: median OS 49 months vs 29.3 months (Bonner). However, multiple studies show cetuximab + RT is inferior to cisplatin + RT in fit patients (RTOG 1016, De-ESCALaTE). Best reserved for cisplatin-ineligible patients. Patient is 45yo and likely cisplatin-eligible.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for LA-HNSCC in combination with radiation. Cetuximab is widely available. EGFR overexpression in this patient's tumor is the target.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than cisplatin: no nephrotoxicity or ototoxicity. Main toxicities are acneiform rash (87%), infusion reactions (18%, 3% severe), radiation dermatitis. Rash correlates with response.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has EGFR overexpression, which is the target of cetuximab (anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody). EGFR expression is common in HNSCC (80-90%). Response does not require EGFR mutation, but overexpression may enhance benefit.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cetuximab is a chimeric IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting EGFR (ErbB1/HER1), blocking ligand binding, inhibiting receptor-mediated signaling (RAS-RAF-MAPK and PI3K-AKT pathways), and inducing receptor internalization and degradation. Also mediates antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC). Radiosensitizes by blocking EGFR-mediated DNA repair.

key_evidence.study_name: Bonner trial / Xiong et al. sinonasal cetuximab-RT study

biomarker_requirements: EGFR expression (present in 80-90% HNSCC — not routinely required for treatment)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — for LA-HNSCC with radiation or R/M HNSCC

treatments.name: Infrastructure Maxillectomy + Adjuvant Cisplatin-Based Chemoradiation (66-70 Gy IMRT)

evidence_level.rationale: No phase 3 RCTs specific to maxillary sinus SCC, but NCCN Category 2A recommendation based on retrospective series and extrapolation from oral cavity/oropharynx phase 3 data. Multiple institutional series (n=40-120) confirm surgery + adjuvant CRT as standard for T4a disease.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS for T4a maxillary sinus SCC ranges 22-40% with multimodal therapy. PMC9251351 reports 22% 5-year OS across all stages (83.7% stage IV). Surgery + adjuvant RT achieves 5-year OS of 62.2% in selected orbital invasion cases per orbital preservation studies.

accessibility.rationale: Surgery and cisplatin/RT are universally available at tertiary head and neck cancer centers. Requires experienced skull base surgeon for infrastructure maxillectomy. Obturator prosthesis fabrication requires maxillofacial prosthodontist.

safety_profile.rationale: Major surgery with significant morbidity: facial disfigurement, palatal defect requiring obturator prosthesis, speech/swallowing impairment, possible orbital exenteration (vision loss). Cisplatin toxicity includes nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, grade 3+ mucositis in 40-60%.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirement — standard of care for all comers with resectable T4a maxillary sinus SCC regardless of molecular profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Infrastructure maxillectomy removes the maxillary sinus tumor with en bloc resection of the hard palate, medial/lateral/posterior maxillary walls, and orbital floor (with or without periorbita). Adjuvant cisplatin acts as a radiosensitizer by forming DNA crosslinks, while IMRT delivers conformal radiation to the tumor bed and elective nodal regions.

key_evidence.study_name: Retrospective institutional series — Tata Memorial Centre

treatments.availability: FDA Approved — available at all tertiary head and neck cancer centers

treatments.name: Induction Chemotherapy (TPF: Docetaxel/Cisplatin/5-FU) Followed by Surgery + Adjuvant RT

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 data specific to sinonasal SCC (Abdelmeguid et al. n=123, Ock et al. n=21). TPF is gold standard induction per phase 3 RCTs (TAX 323/324) in general HNSCC. Comprehensive review in PMC10417481 supports IC for orbital preservation in T4a disease.

survival_benefit.rationale: Induction chemotherapy for sinonasal SCC: 57-67% response rates. Murr et al. showed 2-year OS 100% vs 65.3% (p=0.043) and 3-year OS 100% vs 48.4% (p=0.016) for IC vs SOC. Orbital preservation achieved in 85-100% of responders. 5-year OS 65.8% in partial responders.

accessibility.rationale: TPF regimen components (docetaxel, cisplatin, 5-FU) are all FDA-approved generics, universally available. Requires inpatient administration for 5-FU infusion. 3-4 cycles standard.

safety_profile.rationale: TPF carries significant toxicity: grade 3-4 neutropenia in 76%, febrile neutropenia 11%, death from toxic effects 2.3%. Requires G-CSF prophylaxis. Cisplatin nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity. 5-FU mucositis. Treatment delays surgery by 9-12 weeks.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirements. Universal applicability. EGFR overexpression in this patient may enhance taxane sensitivity. TP53 mutation does not preclude platinum response.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Neoadjuvant TPF aims to shrink tumor before definitive surgery, potentially converting unresectable disease to resectable and enabling orbital preservation. Docetaxel stabilizes microtubules preventing cell division, cisplatin crosslinks DNA, and 5-FU inhibits thymidylate synthase disrupting DNA synthesis. Response to IC serves as in vivo chemosensitivity assessment.

key_evidence.study_name: Induction chemotherapy for sinonasal SCC — multi-institutional review

notable_side_effects: treatment delay of surgery by 9-12 weeks
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — all components are generic, universally available

treatments.name: Nivolumab Monotherapy (CheckMate-141 Regimen — Second Line R/M)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT CheckMate-141 (n=361) demonstrated OS benefit vs investigator's choice in platinum-refractory R/M HNSCC (HR 0.70, p=0.01). FDA approved. 2-year update confirms durable benefit. Nearly tripled 24-month OS rate (16.9% vs 6.0%).

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 7.5 months vs 5.1 months — modest absolute benefit of 2.4 months. This is a second-line salvage setting with poor overall prognosis. 24-month OS only 16.9%. Applicable only if primary treatment fails.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for R/M HNSCC progressing on/after platinum. Nivolumab widely available. Relevant as second-line salvage option.

safety_profile.rationale: Significantly fewer grade 3-4 AEs vs standard chemotherapy (13.1% vs 35.1%). Better quality of life profile. Immune-related AEs manageable. Well-suited for patients with residual surgical morbidity.

biomarker_match.rationale: Benefit observed regardless of PD-L1 status in CheckMate-141, though enriched in PD-L1+ tumors. Patient's CPS 10 suggests moderate PD-L1 expression. TP53 mutation may reduce response per TRIUMPH analysis.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab is a fully human IgG4 monoclonal antibody blocking PD-1 on T cells, restoring antitumor immune surveillance. In the checkpoint-inhibited tumor microenvironment, PD-1 blockade reactivates exhausted tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes to recognize and kill cancer cells.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — second-line R/M HNSCC after platinum failure

treatments.name: Eye-Sparing Infrastructure Maxillectomy with Periorbital Preservation + Adjuvant RT

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple retrospective series support orbital preservation when tumor is limited to orbital floor/periorbita without gross orbital fat invasion. Iannetti et al. (n=111) demonstrated no survival detriment with orbital preservation. Turri-Zanoni (PMC7561452) showed 90.3% orbital preservation feasibility.

survival_benefit.rationale: Orbital preservation cohorts: 5-year OS 34.8% vs 27.3% with exenteration (not statistically different, suggests no oncologic compromise). Orbital recurrence rate only 8.6% with preservation vs 12.5% with exenteration. Modern multimodal series report 5-year OS ~62% with surgery + RT.

accessibility.rationale: Available at tertiary skull base surgery centers with experienced head and neck surgeons. Requires intraoperative frozen section analysis of periorbita. Not all centers have sufficient volume/expertise for reliable orbital preservation decisions.

safety_profile.rationale: Major surgery but preserves ipsilateral eye and binocular vision. Reduced morbidity vs orbital exenteration. Still requires obturator prosthesis. Risks include diplopia, enophthalmos, epiphora, infraorbital nerve hypoesthesia. CSF leak risk if cribriform involved.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirement. Decision based on radiographic and intraoperative assessment of orbital invasion depth. Applicable to all molecular subtypes.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Infrastructure maxillectomy with orbital floor resection but preservation of the periorbita, orbital fat, and globe. Tumor limited to orbital floor erosion without frank orbital fat invasion can be completely resected by removing periorbita and reconstructing the orbital floor (titanium mesh or free flap). Intraoperative frozen section of periorbita margin guides decision to preserve vs exenterate.

key_evidence.study_name: Management of orbital invasion in sinonasal SCC: 15 years experience

biomarker_requirements: Orbital invasion limited to floor/periorbita (no gross orbital fat or extraocular muscle invasion)
treatments.availability: Available at tertiary head and neck / skull base surgery centers

treatments.name: Proton Beam Therapy (Adjuvant or Definitive — Orbital/Optic Nerve Sparing)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 data and retrospective series for sinonasal cancers treated with proton therapy. No phase 3 RCTs comparing proton vs photon for sinonasal SCC specifically. American Radium Society 2025 systematic review supports proton therapy consideration. Johns Hopkins 2025 case study confirms favorable outcomes.

survival_benefit.rationale: Proton therapy achieves comparable tumor control to IMRT but with reduced dose to adjacent critical structures (optic nerves, chiasm, brain, contralateral eye). Enables dose escalation to 70-76 Gy to tumor bed. Reduced late toxicity may improve functional outcomes and quality of life.

accessibility.rationale: Proton beam therapy centers are limited globally (~40 in US, ~120 worldwide). Requires referral to specialized center. Insurance coverage variable. Treatment course 6-7 weeks. Significant travel burden for many patients.

safety_profile.rationale: Superior dose conformity reduces radiation to optic nerves, contralateral eye, temporal lobes, and pituitary gland. Low grade 3+ toxicity. Reduced risk of radiation-induced blindness, cognitive decline, and secondary malignancies. Particularly beneficial for young patients (age 45).

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirement. Benefit is physics-based (dose distribution) rather than biology-based. Applicable to all patients with sinonasal tumors near critical structures.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Proton beams deposit energy via the Bragg peak, concentrating radiation dose at tumor depth with rapid falloff beyond the target. This physics advantage reduces dose to adjacent critical structures (optic nerves, chiasm, brain, contralateral orbit) compared to photon IMRT, enabling dose escalation and orbital sparing.

key_evidence.study_name: Long-term outcomes after PBT for sinonasal SCC / American Radium Society Systematic Review
key_evidence.journal: Head & Neck / International Journal of Radiation Oncology

treatments.availability: Available at ~40 US proton centers and ~120 worldwide — limited access

treatments.name: Definitive Chemoradiation (Organ Preservation — Non-Surgical Approach)

evidence_level.rationale: UK multicentre study (Callan et al. 2021) evaluated definitive IMRT for sinonasal SCC. 5-year OS 30.2% and PFS 24.2% with definitive RT/CRT. No phase 3 RCTs comparing surgery vs definitive CRT for maxillary sinus SCC. NCCN lists as alternative for unresectable or medically inoperable.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS of 30.2% with definitive CRT vs 40-62% with surgery + adjuvant RT/CRT in selected series. Inferior oncologic outcomes compared to surgical approaches for resectable T4a disease. Reserved for patients who refuse surgery or are medically inoperable.

accessibility.rationale: Widely available at all radiation oncology centers. Cisplatin-based CRT is universal standard. Avoids major surgery. No maxillofacial prosthodontist needed.

safety_profile.rationale: Avoids surgical morbidity (no maxillectomy, no obturator). But: grade 3+ mucositis in 40-60%, severe dysphagia, xerostomia, osteoradionecrosis of maxilla, radiation-induced visual complications when treating near orbit. Late effects may be permanent.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirement. Universal applicability. Patient's EGFR overexpression may enhance radiosensitivity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: High-dose IMRT (70 Gy in 35 fractions) to the primary tumor and involved regions with concurrent weekly cisplatin (40 mg/m2) or high-dose cisplatin (100 mg/m2 q3w x3). Organ preservation approach avoids maxillectomy, preserving facial contour, palate, and orbit. Relies entirely on radiation and chemosensitization for tumor control.

key_evidence.study_name: UK Multicentre Study — Sinonasal SCC IMRT outcomes

treatments.name: Neoadjuvant Chemoradiation (26-Year Experience Protocol)

evidence_level.rationale: PMC3465876 reports 26-year institutional experience with neoadjuvant chemoradiation for maxillary sinus SCC. Single-institution retrospective data. No randomized comparison. 5-year OS 35% reported. Provides long-term outcome data specific to maxillary sinus.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS of 35% for all patients treated with neoadjuvant CRT + surgery. May improve resectability and margin status. Theoretical advantage of treating micrometastatic disease early. Delays surgery which may be a disadvantage.

accessibility.rationale: Components (cisplatin + radiation) universally available. Sequence (neoadjuvant CRT then surgery) is well-established at major centers. Requires multidisciplinary coordination.

safety_profile.rationale: Cumulative toxicity from CRT followed by major surgery. Increased wound healing complications post-radiation. Grade 3+ mucositis before surgery. Radiation-induced tissue changes complicate surgical planes and reconstruction.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirement. Universal applicability for locally advanced maxillary sinus SCC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Preoperative radiation (50 Gy) with concurrent cisplatin followed by infrastructure maxillectomy. Neoadjuvant approach aims to sterilize microscopic disease at tumor periphery, reduce tumor volume for improved resectability, and treat potential micrometastatic disease early. Post-radiation surgical specimen allows pathologic assessment of treatment response.

key_evidence.study_name: Neoadjuvant CRT in SCC of the Maxillary Sinus: A 26-Year Experience
key_evidence.journal: PMC / International Journal of Radiation Oncology Biology Physics

notable_side_effects: increased surgical morbidity in irradiated field
treatments.availability: Available at tertiary head and neck cancer centers

treatments.name: Neoadjuvant Pembrolizumab + Chemotherapy (Paranasal Sinus SCC Protocol)

evidence_level.rationale: Case reports and small prospective series (Frontiers in Oncology 2024, n=1-5) show feasibility and dramatic responses. Phase 2 trial (NCT — pembrolizumab in locally advanced sinonasal carcinoma) is active. Extrapolated from KEYNOTE-689 phase 3 data in broader HNSCC.

survival_benefit.rationale: Case report in PMC8943646 showed complete regression of advanced maxillary sinus cancer with orbital invasion using combined chemo-immunotherapy at 1-year follow-up. Prospective phase 2 (20 patients) showed 60% ORR and 100% disease control rate with pembrolizumab + nab-paclitaxel + cisplatin.

accessibility.rationale: Off-label use of pembrolizumab for neoadjuvant sinonasal-specific protocol. May be available through clinical trial enrollment. Insurance coverage uncertain for off-label neoadjuvant use outside KEYNOTE-689 indications.

safety_profile.rationale: Combination immunotherapy + chemotherapy has additive toxicity. Grade 3-4 AEs expected in 50-70% of patients. However, neoadjuvant use may reduce surgical extent if tumor downstaged, potentially enabling orbital preservation.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient's PD-L1 CPS 10 supports immunotherapy benefit. EGFR overexpression may respond to platinum-based chemotherapy component. TP53 mutation is a concern for checkpoint inhibitor response but does not preclude treatment.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab combined with platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin or carboplatin + nab-paclitaxel) aims to achieve maximal tumor shrinkage before surgery. Chemotherapy-induced immunogenic cell death releases tumor antigens that synergize with PD-1 blockade. Potential to convert orbital-invading tumors to organ-preserving resections.

key_evidence.study_name: Neoadjuvant immunotherapy + chemotherapy for paranasal sinus SCC

treatments.availability: Clinical Trial / Off-Label — available at major cancer centers

treatments.name: Afatinib (Second-Generation Irreversible EGFR TKI — Second-Line)

evidence_level.rationale: Two phase 3 RCTs: LUX-Head & Neck 1 (n=483) and LUX-Head & Neck 3 (n=340) both met primary PFS endpoints vs methotrexate in R/M HNSCC. Consistent PFS benefit across both trials. Published in Lancet Oncology.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS improvement modest: 2.6 vs 1.7 months (LUX-HN1). No OS benefit demonstrated. Second-line salvage setting with poor prognosis. Would only be relevant if patient recurs after platinum and immunotherapy.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for NSCLC with EGFR mutations; used off-label for HNSCC. Available as oral tablet. Not FDA-approved for HNSCC but used in practice per LUX-HN data.

safety_profile.rationale: High rate of diarrhea (70-91%), acneiform rash (72-84%), and stomatitis (34-73%). Grade 3+ AEs in ~40%. Manageable with dose reduction. Oral administration is convenient. 89% treatment adherence rate.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has EGFR overexpression, making afatinib particularly relevant. Irreversible ErbB family blocker (EGFR/HER2/HER4). EGFR mutations found in 28% of sinonasal SCC, potentially enhancing sensitivity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Afatinib is a second-generation irreversible pan-ErbB tyrosine kinase inhibitor that covalently binds to EGFR (ErbB1), HER2 (ErbB2), and HER4 (ErbB4), blocking all kinase-active ErbB receptor dimers. This broader blockade overcomes resistance mechanisms seen with first-generation reversible EGFR inhibitors like erlotinib.

treatments.name: Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab (Anti-VEGFR/FGFR TKI + Anti-PD-1)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1b/2 (KEYNOTE-146, n=22 HNSCC) showed 40.9% ORR. Phase 3 LEAP-010 improved ORR (46.1% vs 25.4%) and PFS (6.2 vs 2.8 months) but failed to demonstrate OS benefit over pembrolizumab monotherapy. Phase 2 LEAP-009 ongoing.

survival_benefit.rationale: Despite doubling PFS and improving ORR, LEAP-010 showed no OS benefit (median 15.0 vs 17.9 months, numerically favoring pembro monotherapy). Higher response rate may benefit patients needing rapid tumor shrinkage before surgery.

accessibility.rationale: Both drugs individually FDA-approved but combination not approved for HNSCC. Available through clinical trial or off-label use. Lenvatinib is expensive (~$20,000/month). Insurance coverage uncertain.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3-4 AEs in 91% of patients (KEYNOTE-146 HNSCC cohort). Grade 4 AEs in 14%. 18% discontinuation rate due to AEs. Common: hypertension, fatigue, diarrhea, decreased appetite. Significant toxicity burden.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient's PD-L1 CPS 10 supports pembrolizumab component. Lenvatinib targets VEGFR/FGFR which are broadly expressed. EGFR overexpression may correlate with angiogenesis pathway activation. No validated predictive biomarker for combination.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lenvatinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFRα, RET, and KIT, inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and remodeling the tumor microenvironment to enhance immunotherapy efficacy. Combined with pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1), this dual approach normalizes tumor vasculature, increases T-cell infiltration, and blocks immune checkpoints simultaneously.
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial / Off-Label — not FDA-approved for HNSCC combination

treatments.name: TRIUMPH-Guided Therapy (Biomarker-Driven Umbrella Trial for TP53-Mutant HNSCC)

evidence_level.rationale: Multicenter biomarker-driven umbrella trial in Korea evaluating targeted agents (alpelisib, poziotinib, nintedanib, abemaciclib) for molecularly-defined HNSCC subgroups. Phase 2 design. Results from TP53-mutant arm reported in PMC12263242 (2025). Novel precision medicine paradigm.

survival_benefit.rationale: Early phase data — survival benefit not yet established. Anti-PD-1 therapy was less effective in most TP53-mutant patients per TRIUMPH analysis. CDK4/6 inhibitors (abemaciclib) showed signals in cell-cycle dysregulated tumors. Exploratory endpoints.

accessibility.rationale: Available only through TRIUMPH trial enrollment in Korea. Limited to participating centers. Requires comprehensive genomic profiling for arm assignment. Not available for general clinical use.

safety_profile.rationale: Varies by assigned agent. Abemaciclib: diarrhea, neutropenia. Alpelisib: hyperglycemia, rash. Nintedanib: hepatotoxicity, GI effects. Generally manageable grade 1-2 AEs with dose modifications.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient's TP53 mutation is the defining enrollment biomarker. TRIUMPH specifically evaluates therapies selected based on molecular profiling including TP53 status. EGFR overexpression may qualify for additional arms.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Biomarker-driven umbrella trial assigning patients to targeted therapies based on molecular profiling. For TP53-mutant HNSCC, cell-cycle checkpoint inhibitors (CDK4/6 inhibitors like abemaciclib) target the compensatory proliferative pathway. Other arms include PI3K inhibitors (alpelisib) and multi-kinase inhibitors (nintedanib) based on co-occurring molecular alterations.

key_evidence.study_name: TRIUMPH Trial — Biomarker-Driven Umbrella Trial for HNSCC

biomarker_requirements: Comprehensive genomic profiling required for arm assignment
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial Only — TRIUMPH umbrella trial (Korea)

treatments.name: Wee-1 Inhibitor (Adavosertib/AZD1775) — TP53-Mutant Targeting Strategy

evidence_level.rationale: Early phase 1/2 data in TP53-mutant solid tumors including HNSCC. Prioritized agent per JNCI review of TP53-mutant HNSCC clinical trial development. No completed phase 3 trials. Basket trials in development combining Wee-1 inhibition with chemotherapy or immunotherapy.

survival_benefit.rationale: Preclinical and early clinical data show synthetic lethality in TP53-mutant tumors lacking G1 checkpoint. Phase 1 data shows tumor shrinkage in TP53-mutant HNSCC. Survival benefit not yet quantified in trials. Potential to address the unmet need of TP53-mutant tumors resistant to immunotherapy.

accessibility.rationale: Available only through clinical trials. Limited enrollment sites. Next-generation Wee-1 inhibitors (ZN-c3) in development with improved pharmacology. Not commercially available.

safety_profile.rationale: Dose-limiting toxicities include myelosuppression (thrombocytopenia, neutropenia), fatigue, nausea. Adavosertib development partially limited by toxicity profile. Newer agents (ZN-c3) designed to improve therapeutic window.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has TP53 mutation — the key biomarker for Wee-1 inhibitor synthetic lethality. TP53-mutant tumors depend entirely on the G2/M checkpoint (Wee-1 mediated) for DNA damage repair, creating vulnerability. Precision medicine approach directly targeting the patient's molecular alteration.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Wee-1 kinase inhibitors exploit synthetic lethality in TP53-mutant tumors. Normal cells use both G1 (p53-mediated) and G2/M (Wee-1-mediated) checkpoints for DNA repair. TP53-mutant cells lose the G1 checkpoint and become entirely dependent on Wee-1 for the G2/M checkpoint. Inhibiting Wee-1 forces TP53-mutant cells into mitosis with unrepaired DNA damage, causing mitotic catastrophe and cell death.

key_evidence.study_name: TP53-mutant HNSCC clinical trial development — JNCI review / Phase 1 basket trials
key_evidence.journal: JNCI: Journal of the National Cancer Institute

clinical_trials.title: Perioperative Pembrolizumab + Surgery + Adjuvant Therapy for Resectable Locally Advanced HNSCC
clinical_trials.early_results: EFS HR 0.73 (p=0.0041). Median EFS 51.8 vs 30.4 months. 36-month EFS 57.6% vs 46.4%. No negative impact on surgical completion. First positive perioperative trial in HNSCC in >20 years.

clinical_trials.title: Pembrolizumab in Locally Advanced Sinonasal Carcinoma
biomarker_requirements: Locally advanced sinonasal carcinoma (includes maxillary sinus SCC)
clinical_trials.early_results: Enrollment ongoing. Evaluating neoadjuvant pembrolizumab followed by surgery and adjuvant therapy specifically for sinonasal malignancies.

clinical_trials.title: Induction Chemotherapy Followed by Surgery vs Upfront Surgery for Resectable Sinonasal Carcinoma
clinical_trials.early_results: 54% partial response in SNSCC cohort with induction chemotherapy. 5-year OS 46% across all pathologies. 100% orbital preservation in IC responders. Evaluating IC role in organ preservation.

clinical_trials.title: Ficlatuzumab With or Without Cetuximab in Recurrent/Metastatic HNSCC
clinical_trials.early_results: Evaluating dual EGFR + HGF/cMET pathway blockade. Rationale based on EGFR-overexpressing tumors developing cMET-mediated resistance. Relevant for EGFR+ maxillary sinus SCC.

clinical_trials.title: Biomarker-Driven Umbrella Trial for Advanced HNSCC — TP53-Mutant Arms
clinical_trials.early_results: Evaluating targeted agents (alpelisib, poziotinib, nintedanib, abemaciclib) in molecularly-defined HNSCC subgroups. TP53-mutant arm analyzing synthetic lethality strategies.

clinical_trials.title: Lenvatinib +/- Pembrolizumab vs Chemotherapy for R/M HNSCC After Platinum + Immunotherapy
clinical_trials.early_results: Evaluating lenvatinib + pembrolizumab as post-immunotherapy salvage option. Phase 1b/2 showed 40.9% ORR. Phase 3 LEAP-010 showed PFS benefit but no OS advantage.

combination_strategies.rationale: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab (2 cycles) primes antitumor immunity before surgery, potentially improving surgical margins and enabling orbital preservation through tumor shrinkage. Adjuvant pembrolizumab (15 cycles) with CRT extends immune surveillance post-resection. Patient's PD-L1 CPS 10 meets eligibility. EFS HR 0.73 demonstrated.

Adding cetuximab to platinum-taxane induction achieves 96% ORR in maxillary sinus SCC (PMC10823329). Patient's EGFR overexpression provides the molecular target. Aims to maximize tumor downstaging before surgery for orbital preservation. Well-tolerated induction option even with comorbidities.

Proton beam therapy enables dose escalation (70-76 Gy) while reducing dose to optic nerves, contralateral eye, temporal lobes, and brain. Particularly beneficial for this 45-year-old with T4a orbital floor invasion where sparing the ipsilateral optic nerve and contralateral orbit is critical for long-term quality of life. American Radium Society 2025 review supports this approach.

If disease recurs after primary treatment, pembrolizumab + cisplatin/carboplatin + 5-FU is first-line standard for R/M HNSCC. Patient's CPS 10 falls in CPS >= 1 subgroup with demonstrated OS benefit (HR 0.64). Chemotherapy-induced immunogenic cell death synergizes with PD-1 blockade.

Patient's TP53 mutation creates synthetic lethality with Wee-1 inhibition. Adding cisplatin generates DNA damage that TP53-mutant cells cannot repair at G1 checkpoint — combined with Wee-1 blockade at G2/M, forces mitotic catastrophe. JNCI review prioritizes this combination for clinical trial development in TP53-mutant HNSCC.

supportive_care.evidence: Multiple studies (PMC8657009, n=25; PMC5795281, comprehensive review). Quality of life after obturator rehabilitation averages 81.48%. Staged approach: surgical obturator → immediate obturator → definitive obturator with progressive functional improvement.
supportive_care.benefit: Restores speech intelligibility, swallowing function, and facial contour after maxillectomy. Most significant predictors of success: extent of soft palate resection (<=1/3) and hard palate resection (<=1/4). Retention and stability strongly correlate with QoL. Digital planning (2025) enables immediate obturator fabrication.
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Strong — essential component of post-maxillectomy rehabilitation

supportive_care.approach: Nutritional Support and PEG/NG Tube Management During CRT
supportive_care.evidence: Frontiers in Oncology 2024 systematic review. Early nutritional intervention reduces severe oral mucositis incidence. Intensive nutritional counseling improves treatment adherence and reduces toxicity-related treatment interruptions. 30-50% of non-surgically treated H&N patients develop swallowing dysfunction.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevents malnutrition-related treatment delays, maintains body composition during chemoradiation, reduces grade 3+ mucositis. Prophylactic PEG placement recommended for anticipated 6-7 week CRT course. Caloric goals: 25-30 kcal/kg/day with 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day protein.
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Strong — ESPEN/NCCN recommended for all H&N CRT patients

supportive_care.evidence: Systematic review and meta-analysis of RCTs (ScienceDirect 2020). Swallowing exercises during and after H&N cancer treatment improve functional outcomes. Home-based telehealth services (PubMed 29081270) show comparable outcomes to in-person rehabilitation.
supportive_care.benefit: Pre-habilitation swallowing exercises before CRT reduce post-treatment dysphagia severity. Mendelsohn maneuver, effortful swallow, and Shaker exercises maintain pharyngeal muscle function during radiation. Post-maxillectomy speech therapy specifically addresses resonance changes from palatal defect.
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Strong — standard of care at all H&N cancer centers

supportive_care.approach: Psychosocial Support and Facial Disfigurement Counseling
supportive_care.evidence: H&N cancer patients have highest rates of depression and suicide among cancer types. Maxillectomy with facial disfigurement significantly impacts body image, social functioning, and relationship quality. Young age (45) amplifies psychosocial impact.
supportive_care.benefit: Pre-surgical counseling improves post-operative adjustment. Peer support groups with maxillectomy survivors. Cognitive behavioral therapy for body image disturbance. Occupational rehabilitation planning given woodworking exposure and potential need for career change to avoid further wood dust exposure.
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Strong — NCCN distress management guidelines recommend screening and intervention

supportive_care.approach: Occupational Health Intervention — Wood Dust Exposure Cessation
supportive_care.evidence: IARC Group 1 carcinogen classification for wood dust. Meta-analysis (PLOS One, PMC4507857): OR 11.9 for sinonasal cancer in wood/paper industry workers. Risk persists for >=15 years after exposure cessation. NCI and NCBI Bookshelf (NBK590780, NBK304376) confirm established carcinogenicity.
supportive_care.benefit: Cessation of wood dust exposure eliminates ongoing carcinogenic stimulus. Reduces risk of second primary sinonasal malignancy. If patient cannot change occupation, N95 mask or powered air-purifying respirator (PAPR) mandatory. Workplace dust extraction systems per OSHA standards. Regular surveillance imaging recommended.

supportive_care.evidence: Pre-radiation dental assessment reduces osteoradionecrosis (ORN) risk from 15-20% to <5%. Fluoride tray application prevents radiation caries. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBO) for ORN prevention in high-dose radiation fields controversial but used at some centers.
supportive_care.benefit: Pre-CRT dental extractions of compromised teeth in radiation field prevent post-radiation ORN. Custom fluoride trays for lifelong daily fluoride application. Remaining teeth serve as obturator retention anchors — preservation critical for prosthetic rehabilitation.
